title: A Guide to Airbnb's Resolution Center

How to Handle Difficult Guests as an Airbnb Host

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.bio: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

reviewedBy.name: Priya Singh, Esq.
reviewedBy.bio: Priya is an attorney specializing in international contract law for independent contractors. She ensures that the legal advice provided is accurate, actionable, and up-to-date with current regulations.
credentials: Member of the New York State Bar

key_takeaways: Establish an undeniable baseline for every stay by recording a narrated, timestamped video walkthrough of your property's condition and key assets.
Maintain a 'Replacement Value Ledger' with receipts and product links to preemptively justify the cost of any damaged items and eliminate debates over value.
When filing a claim, communicate exclusively on-platform and present your evidence to the mediator as a simple, chronological timeline that references each document by file name.

As a professional guest, document the property's condition and verify critical amenities like Wi-Fi speed with timestamped evidence upon arrival to protect yourself if the listing is not as advertised.
summary: To win Airbnb damage disputes, hosts must shift from a reactive to a proactive approach by building an ironclad case before a guest ever arrives. The core advice is to create timestamped pre-stay video walkthroughs, frame house rules with clear financial consequences, and maintain a replacement value ledger for all assets. By following a strict protocol of documenting damage and communicating professionally on-platform, hosts can transform an emotional confrontation into a simple business transaction, presenting a clear, evidence-based claim that makes it easy for a mediator to rule in their favor.
ai_overview_answer:
To successfully win an Airbnb damage claim, hosts must proactively build an evidence-based case before the stay and follow a strict documentation protocol after an incident. The process begins by creating a pre-stay, timestamped video walkthrough to establish the property's initial condition and maintaining a "Replacement Value Ledger" with receipts for key items. If damage occurs, immediately document the scene with new timestamped photos and videos before cleaning. All communication with the guest regarding the incident must be conducted exclusively through the Airbnb messaging platform to serve as valid evidence.

Quantify the loss with professional repair quotes or original receipts and file a claim in the Resolution Center within 14 days of checkout. When presenting the case, use a simple timeline format and reference each piece of evidence by its file name. This methodical, evidence-based approach removes emotion and makes it easy for an Airbnb mediator to verify the claim and rule in your favor.

itemListElement.name: A Guide to Airbnb's Resolution Center

@graph.headline: A Guide to Airbnb's Resolution Center
@graph.description: To win Airbnb damage disputes, hosts must shift from a reactive to a proactive approach by building an ironclad case before a guest ever arrives. The core advice is to create timestamped pre-stay video walkthroughs, frame house rules with clear financial consequences, and maintain a replacement value ledger for all assets. By following a strict protocol of documenting damage and communicating professionally on-platform, hosts can transform an emotional confrontation into a simple business transaction, presenting a clear, evidence-based claim that makes it easy for a mediator to rule in their favor.

author.name: Elena Petrova
author.description: An international business lawyer by trade, Elena breaks down the complexities of freelance contracts, corporate structures, and international liability. Her goal is to empower freelancers with the legal knowledge to operate confidently.

step.name: Secure the Scene & Document Immediately.
step.text: Before you touch, move, or clean anything, treat the area like a forensic scene. Your goal is to capture the situation exactly as you found it. Create a New Timestamped Record: Take wide-angle photos and videos to establish context, then move in for detailed close-ups from multiple angles. Narrate a "Post-Stay" Walkthrough: Mirror your pre-stay process. Speak calmly and factually: "Living room, October 17th, post-checkout. The coffee table now has a 12-inch crack across the surface, which was not present in the pre-stay video from October 14th." Show the "Before and After": For maximum impact, hold up your phone displaying the pre-stay photo of the item in its original condition next to the actual damage and record a short video of the two. This visual comparison is incredibly compelling.

step.name: Initiate Professional Communication (On-Platform).
step.text: Your first contact with the guest about the issue must be through the Airbnb messaging system. Moving the conversation to text or email invalidates it as evidence. Keep the tone factual, firm, and non-accusatory; you are a business operator reporting a loss, not a person starting a fight. Example Message: "Hi [Guest Name], hope you had a safe trip home. During my post-stay inspection, I discovered significant damage to the induction stovetop that was not present prior to your arrival. I have documented this with timestamped photos and video. Per my house rules, I will need to file a claim to cover the replacement. Could you provide any details about what happened?"

step.name: Quantify the Loss Objectively.
step.text: This is where your Replacement Value Ledger becomes invaluable. Present a clear, evidence-based calculation of the financial loss, removing all subjectivity. Gather Your Proof: Pull the original receipt or the direct product link from your ledger. For repairs, get a formal quote or invoice from a qualified professional. Present the Numbers: When you open your case, you will attach these documents. This turns a potential argument over an item's value into a simple matter of reimbursement for a documented cost.

step.name: File in the Resolution Center Within 14 Days.
step.text: Airbnb has a strict deadline: you must file your reimbursement request in the Resolution Center within 14 days of the guest's checkout to be eligible for reimbursement under AirCover for Hosts. Waiting longer, even while negotiating with the guest, can invalidate your claim. File Promptly: Go to airbnb.com/resolutions to begin. The best practice is to file as soon as you have documented the damage and quantified the loss. The Guest's Response Window: Once submitted, the guest has a set time to respond. If they decline or ignore the request, you can then escalate the case by clicking "Involve Airbnb," which sends your meticulously organized file to a mediator.

mainEntity.name: What is the best evidence for an Airbnb damage claim?

acceptedAnswer.text: An undeniable "before and after" narrative. This requires four key elements: 1) A pre-stay, timestamped video walkthrough to establish the baseline condition. 2) Post-stay, timestamped photos and videos isolating the specific damage. 3) Objective proof of value, such as an original receipt, a link to an identical replacement item, or a professional repair estimate. 4) Unedited screenshots of your on-platform communication with the guest.

mainEntity.name: How do you write an effective message in the Airbnb Resolution Center?

acceptedAnswer.text: Be brief, factual, and professional. Use a simple timeline to establish the sequence of events and reference each piece of evidence by its file name. State your request clearly. Example: "On Oct 15, 'Pre-Stay_Video.mp4' shows the wall was unmarked. On Oct 17, 'Damage_Photos.zip' show a large scuff. 'Painter_Invoice.pdf' details the $175 repair cost. I am respectfully requesting $175."

mainEntity.name: What happens if a guest ignores a Resolution Center request?

acceptedAnswer.text: A guest's silence triggers the next step. After a set response time (typically 24-72 hours), the option to "Involve Airbnb" becomes available in your case file. At this point, you can escalate the issue to an Airbnb case manager, who will make a binding decision based on the evidence you have provided.

mainEntity.name: Can you use the Resolution Center after leaving a review?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes. The two processes operate on separate timelines. The review period is 14 days from checkout. The window to submit a reimbursement request via the Resolution Center (and be eligible for AirCover for Hosts) is also within 14 days of checkout. You can, and often should, leave your honest review of the guest and proceed with your Resolution Center request concurrently.

mainEntity.name: How long does the Airbnb Resolution Center take?

acceptedAnswer.text: The timeline has two phases. The initial phase is the guest's response window. If you must involve Airbnb for mediation, the second phase begins. While many straightforward cases are resolved in under a week, more complex disputes can take longer. The quality and organization of your submitted evidence is the most significant factor influencing speed.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the Resolution Center and AirCover for Hosts?

acceptedAnswer.text: This is a critical distinction. The Resolution Center is the tool—the official on-platform channel you must use to request money from a guest. AirCover for Hosts is the protection program—Airbnb's host damage protection. To be eligible for reimbursement under AirCover for Hosts, you must first go through the Resolution Center. If the guest refuses to pay and Airbnb approves your claim, AirCover for Hosts is the mechanism that may provide reimbursement.

body.content: Before the Dispute: Your Pre-Stay Protocol for an Ironclad Case

Professional confidence begins long before a guest checks in. It starts with building a fortress of evidence so strong that most disputes are won before they are ever filed. This pre-emptive protocol transforms you from a reactive victim into a proactive operator, creating an unassailable baseline of evidence that an Airbnb mediator cannot ignore. This is your first and most effective line of defense.

items: **Establish a "Condition Library."** Before every stay, conduct a full, timestamped video walkthrough of the property. Do not just sweep the room with your camera; narrate the process like a property inspector. State the date, then move through each space, verbally confirming the condition of key items. "Living room, October 17th. Sofa is clean, no stains or tears. Television, model number XYZ, is powering on and fully functional." Capture close-ups of serial numbers on expensive electronics. This video is your non-negotiable proof of the property's initial state—your baseline for reality—and it single-handedly eliminates the "he said, she said" dynamic.

**Frame House Rules as Enforceable Clauses.** Standard house rules are often ignored because they lack consequences. To protect your business, frame them for clear financial enforceability. Vague suggestions invite debate; contractual clauses demand compliance.

rows: "Unregistered guests are not permitted on the property at any time. Evidence of unregistered guests will result in a $250 fee for additional cleaning, security, and amenity usage, as agreed upon by booking this property."
"This is a strictly non-smoking property. Evidence of smoking (including e-cigarettes) will incur a $500 deep-cleaning and deodorizing fee."

items: **Create a Replacement Value Ledger.** When an item is damaged, the debate over its value can derail your claim. Preempt this by maintaining a simple spreadsheet listing your key assets—appliances, electronics, expensive linens, and furniture. For each item, log its name, purchase date, price, and a direct link to the product listing or a digital copy of the receipt. When you file a claim, you are not pulling a number out of thin air; you are presenting a documented, indisputable business loss.

**The Professional Guest's Protocol.** Risk management is a two-way street. As a global professional, your own work can depend on the amenities promised. Upon arrival at any property, take five minutes to document its state. Run a Wi-Fi speed test and screenshot the results—proof if "high-speed internet" fails during a critical video call. Take timestamped photos of your workspace and the general condition of the unit. This is not paranoia; it is professional diligence, providing the evidence you need if a critical amenity is not as advertised.

body.content: The Incident Response Protocol: 4 Steps When Things Go Wrong

Your pre-emptive work creates the foundation, but your actions in the first few hours after discovering an incident are what activate that preparation. When you find a shattered stovetop or a wine-stained sofa, a methodical response is paramount. This protocol ensures you control the process, create an unimpeachable paper trail, and build a powerful case for the Airbnb Resolution Center.

items:
**Step 1: Secure the Scene & Document Immediately.**
Before you touch, move, or clean anything, treat the area like a forensic scene. Your goal is to capture the situation exactly as you found it.
*   **Create a New Timestamped Record:** Take wide-angle photos and videos to establish context, then move in for detailed close-ups from multiple angles.
*   **Narrate a "Post-Stay" Walkthrough:** Mirror your pre-stay process. Speak calmly and factually: "Living room, October 17th, post-checkout. The coffee table now has a 12-inch crack across the surface, which was not present in the pre-stay video from October 14th."
*   **Show the "Before and After":** For maximum impact, hold up your phone displaying the pre-stay photo of the item in its original condition next to the actual damage and record a short video of the two. This visual comparison is incredibly compelling.

**Step 2: Initiate Professional Communication (On-Platform).**
Your first contact with the guest about the issue *must* be through the Airbnb messaging system. Moving the conversation to text or email invalidates it as evidence. Keep the tone factual, firm, and non-accusatory; you are a business operator reporting a loss, not a person starting a fight.
*   **Example Message:** *"Hi [Guest Name], hope you had a safe trip home. During my post-stay inspection, I discovered significant damage to the induction stovetop that was not present prior to your arrival. I have documented this with timestamped photos and video. Per my house rules, I will need to file a claim to cover the replacement. Could you provide any details about what happened?"*

**Step 3: Quantify the Loss Objectively.**
This is where your Replacement Value Ledger becomes invaluable. Present a clear, evidence-based calculation of the financial loss, removing all subjectivity.
*   **Gather Your Proof:** Pull the original receipt or the direct product link from your ledger. For repairs, get a formal quote or invoice from a qualified professional.
*   **Present the Numbers:** When you open your case, you will attach these documents. This turns a potential argument over an item's value into a simple matter of reimbursement for a documented cost.

**Step 4: File in the Resolution Center Within 14 Days.**
Airbnb has a strict deadline: you must file your reimbursement request in the Resolution Center within 14 days of the guest's checkout to be eligible for reimbursement under AirCover for Hosts. Waiting longer, even while negotiating with the guest, can invalidate your claim.
*   **File Promptly:** Go to airbnb.com/resolutions to begin. The best practice is to file as soon as you have documented the damage and quantified the loss.
*   **The Guest's Response Window:** Once submitted, the guest has a set time to respond. If they decline or ignore the request, you can then escalate the case by clicking "Involve Airbnb," which sends your meticulously organized file to a mediator.

body.content: Presenting Your Case: Communication in the Resolution Center

Once you file a request, your communication style must shift. You are no longer just messaging a guest; you are building a formal case that a neutral third-party mediator will review. Every word should demonstrate professionalism, substantiate your claim, and make it simple for the mediator to rule in your favor.
Adopt a "Factual, Firm, and Fair" Tone

Emotion is the enemy of resolution. Venting frustration undermines your credibility. A mediator is looking for an objective business operator, not a personally offended homeowner. Focus on the business impact and the documented facts.

items: **Avoid:** *"I can't believe how disrespectful you were. You completely trashed my coffee table and didn't even have the decency to tell me."*

**Instead, use:** *"The post-stay inspection revealed significant damage to the living room coffee table, which was not present prior to your stay. This damage requires a full replacement to return the property to its original condition."*

body.content: Use a Timeline Format

Mediators review multiple cases at once. Make their job easier by presenting the facts in a simple, chronological timeline. This structure methodically builds your case from an undisputed starting point to a documented loss.

items: **October 14, 11:05 AM:** Pre-stay video walkthrough completed. Sofa documented at 03:45 in perfect condition, with no stains.
**October 14, 4:00 PM:** Guest checks in.
**October 17, 10:00 AM:** Guest checks out.
**October 17, 12:15 PM:** Post-stay inspection conducted. A large red wine stain is discovered on the main sofa cushion.
**October 17, 12:30 PM:** Photos and video of the damage are taken and timestamped.
**October 17, 1:00 PM:** Professional cleaning service contacted for a quote.

body.content: Reference Your Evidence In-Line

Do not simply upload a folder of files and expect the mediator to connect the dots. Act as the guide. Explicitly reference each piece of evidence within your written statements to create an undeniable link between your claim and your proof.

items:
**Example Phrases:**
*   *"As seen in 'Pre-Stay_Video.mp4' at the 02:15 mark, the television screen was undamaged upon guest arrival."*
*   *"The replacement cost is verified in the attached 'Receipt_TV_Purchase.pdf', confirming the value at $550."*
*   *"Our on-platform conversation, documented in 'Message_Screenshot.png', shows my attempt to resolve this directly with the guest on October 17th."*

body.content: State Your Desired Outcome Clearly
Conclude your message with a concise and unambiguous request. The mediator should not have to guess what you are asking for.

items: **Example Closing:** *"Therefore, based on the documented damage and the attached invoice for professional stain removal, I am requesting the full amount of $275 to cover the cost of the service."*

body.content: The Escalation Protocol: How to Win with a Mediator

When a guest declines or ignores your request, you are forced to escalate. This is not the moment you lose control; it is the moment you present your prepared case to a neutral arbitrator. Your entire focus should be on making the mediator’s job simple. Provide a brief so clear, logical, and well-supported that ruling in your favor is their easiest and most obvious choice.

items:
**Organize Your Evidence into a "Case File."** Before you click "Involve Airbnb," consolidate your documentation into a single folder. This includes your pre-stay video, incident photos, communication screenshots, and receipts or invoices. Use a clear, sequential naming convention that tells the story on its own:
*   `1_Pre-Stay_Video.mp4`
*   `2_Damage_Photos.zip`
*   `3_Guest_Communication_Screenshots.pdf`
*   `4_Replacement_Invoice.pdf`

**Write a Final Summary for the Mediator.** In the text box provided, write a concise summary as if you were presenting to a judge. Re-state your timeline, reference each piece of evidence by its file name, cite the specific house rule that was broken, and confirm the exact financial amount requested. As New York City–based attorney Francine Levitov advises, "When the judge asks you to present your story, get to the point... The most common mistake that small claims litigants make is confusing arguing a case with being personally argumentative."

**Leverage Airbnb's Own Policies.** Frame your argument within the platform's ecosystem. Explicitly reference Airbnb's policies, like the Guest Reliability Standards, or the protections offered under AirCover for Hosts. Using a phrase like, *"As per Airbnb's policy on property damage, we are submitting this claim for reimbursement,"* demonstrates that you are an informed, professional partner operating within their established rules.

**Maintain Professional Composure.** Once you escalate, your direct work is done. Resist the urge to engage in any further back-and-forth with the guest. Trust the case file you have built and communicate only with the Airbnb mediator. Answer any direct questions they have promptly, factually, and without emotion. Your calm, business-like demeanor solidifies your credibility and allows the evidence to speak for itself.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**What is the best evidence for an Airbnb damage claim?**
An undeniable "before and after" narrative. This requires four key elements: 1) A pre-stay, timestamped video walkthrough to establish the baseline condition. 2) Post-stay, timestamped photos and videos isolating the specific damage. 3) Objective proof of value, such as an original receipt, a link to an identical replacement item, or a professional repair estimate. 4) Unedited screenshots of your on-platform communication with the guest.

**How do you write an effective message in the Airbnb Resolution Center?**
Be brief, factual, and professional. Use a simple timeline to establish the sequence of events and reference each piece of evidence by its file name. State your request clearly. Example: *"On Oct 15, 'Pre-Stay_Video.mp4' shows the wall was unmarked. On Oct 17, 'Damage_Photos.zip' show a large scuff. 'Painter_Invoice.pdf' details the $175 repair cost. I am respectfully requesting $175."*

**What happens if a guest ignores a Resolution Center request?**
A guest's silence triggers the next step. After a set response time (typically 24-72 hours), the option to "Involve Airbnb" becomes available in your case file. At this point, you can escalate the issue to an Airbnb case manager, who will make a binding decision based on the evidence you have provided.

**Can you use the Resolution Center after leaving a review?**
Yes. The two processes operate on separate timelines. The review period is 14 days from checkout. The window to submit a reimbursement request via the Resolution Center (and be eligible for AirCover for Hosts) is also within 14 days of checkout. You can, and often should, leave your honest review of the guest and proceed with your Resolution Center request concurrently.

**How long does the Airbnb Resolution Center take?**
The timeline has two phases. The initial phase is the guest's response window. If you must involve Airbnb for mediation, the second phase begins. While many straightforward cases are resolved in under a week, more complex disputes can take longer. The quality and organization of your submitted evidence is the most significant factor influencing speed.

**What is the difference between the Resolution Center and AirCover for Hosts?**
This is a critical distinction. The **Resolution Center** is the *tool*—the official on-platform channel you must use to request money from a guest. **AirCover for Hosts** is the *protection program*—Airbnb's host damage protection. To be eligible for reimbursement under AirCover for Hosts, you **must** first go through the Resolution Center. If the guest refuses to pay and Airbnb approves your claim, AirCover for Hosts is the mechanism that may provide reimbursement.

body.content: Conclusion: From Operator to Strategist

This entire framework—from pre-stay videos to incident response protocols—is designed to cultivate a fundamental shift in your professional mindset. You are transitioning from a host who hopes for the best into a strategic operator who plans for the worst. This is the difference between anxiety and control.

This protocol-driven approach systematically dismantles the stress of guest disputes. When an issue arises, you no longer feel a surge of panic. Instead, you feel the calm certainty of knowing exactly what to do next. You have a playbook. Your response becomes a series of deliberate actions: document, communicate, quantify, and file. Each step turns a potentially emotional confrontation into a simple business transaction.

With this system, the Resolution Center is demystified. It ceases to be an adversarial arena and becomes an administrative tool with clear rules. By consistently providing high-quality, organized, and indisputable evidence, you make it easy for a mediator to arrive at the logical, favorable conclusion. You are not leaving the outcome to chance; you are structuring the narrative to make your desired outcome the most direct path for the decision-maker.

Ultimately, mastering this process is not the end goal. The true victory is the operational freedom this mastery provides. By standardizing your approach to risk, you quarantine the entire issue, preventing it from consuming your time and energy. You are free to concentrate on the activities that actually grow your business: enhancing the guest experience, optimizing your listings, and scaling your portfolio. You operate with the quiet confidence of a professional who has a system for everything—including the unexpected.
